title: PDF attachments are corrupted after upload
body: When customers upload PDF files to their tasks, the files come through broken. You can't open them — the PDF viewer says the file is damaged. This is happening for multiple customers. We use the file attachment feature heavily so this is really impacting our workflow.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for reporting this — corrupted attachments sound really disruptive, especially if your team relies on them heavily.

To start understanding what's going on: can you walk me through what the process looks like when this happens? For example, what are you doing right before the upload — are you dragging files in, using an upload button, or something else? And once the upload finishes, does everything *look* normal in TaskFlow until you try to download and open the file, or are there signs something went wrong during the upload itself?

Also, do you happen to know if the original PDF files on your end are still fine (i.e., you can open them locally without issues)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Look, the original files are fine on our end. We can open them no problem before uploading. We just use the regular upload button, nothing fancy. It looks like it uploads fine — no errors or anything — but then when someone downloads it, it's garbage. Can't open it. This started maybe a week ago? We need this fixed, our team is constantly sharing PDFs with clients.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful, thank you. The timing is interesting — you mentioned this started about a week ago. Before that, were PDF uploads working fine for your team? And do you recall if anything else changed around that time — maybe a browser update, or did your team notice any other differences in how TaskFlow was behaving?

Also, I'm curious: does this happen with *every* PDF you upload now, or are some getting through okay? For instance, have you noticed any pattern with file size or which team member is uploading?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah it was working fine before, nothing changed on our end. No browser updates or anything like that. And now that you mention it... I think some smaller PDFs might be going through okay? I'm not totally sure but I feel like the ones that are messing up tend to be the bigger files. But honestly I haven't been tracking it closely, I just know it's broken and we need it fixed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That observation about file size is really useful — even a rough sense helps narrow things down. Could you try a quick test for me? Take one of the PDFs that's been coming through corrupted and check how large it is (right-click → Properties or Get Info). Even a ballpark like "a few MB" vs. "20+ MB" would help.

And one more thing I'm curious about: does your team ever attach other types of files — like Word docs, images, or spreadsheets? If so, are those coming through fine, or is the corruption happening across the board? That'll help us figure out whether this is something specific to how PDFs are being handled or a broader upload issue.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I don't have time to run tests for you, I have a team to manage. But the ones that are broken are definitely bigger files — like multi-page reports and stuff, probably a few MB each. The little one-pagers seem to be okay I think. As for other files... actually yeah, now that you mention it, someone did say an image they uploaded looked weird too, but I figured that was a separate thing. Can you just fix the PDF issue? That's what's killing us right now.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Large file attachments corrupted after upload/download — started ~1 week ago, affects PDFs and images","problem":"Files uploaded via the task attachment feature are corrupted when downloaded. The corruption correlates with file size: larger files (multi-page PDFs, several MB) are consistently broken, while small single-page files appear to come through intact. At least one image upload has also been affected, indicating this is not PDF-specific. No errors are shown during upload. The issue began approximately one week ago with no client-side changes.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A server-side or infrastructure change approximately one week ago is corrupting larger file uploads or downloads. Likely candidates: (1) a change in multipart upload handling that truncates or re-encodes files above a certain size, (2) a reverse proxy, CDN, or gateway configuration change introducing a body size limit or timeout that silently truncates responses, (3) a change in content-type or transfer-encoding handling (e.g., binary vs base64) that mangles non-text payloads, or (4) a storage layer change affecting how file blobs are written or read.","reproduction_steps":["Upload a multi-page PDF (~2-5 MB) to a task using the standard upload button","Observe that the upload completes without errors","Download the same file from the task","Attempt to open the downloaded PDF — it will be reported as damaged/corrupted","Repeat with a small single-page PDF (~100 KB) — this may succeed","Repeat with a larger image file to confirm cross-format corruption"],"environment":"Multiple customers affected, standard upload button used, no specific browser/OS identified but issue is cross-user. Production environment, onset approximately 2026-03-31.","severity":"high","impact":"Teams that rely on file attachments for client-facing document sharing are blocked. Multiple customers affected. Workaround is limited to sharing only very small files.","recommended_fix":"1. Review all deployments and infrastructure changes from approximately one week ago (late March 2026). 2. Compare byte-for-byte a known-corrupted uploaded file against its original to identify the corruption pattern (truncation, encoding change, null bytes, etc.). 3. Check upload/download pipeline for size-dependent behavior: proxy body limits, chunked transfer handling, streaming vs buffering thresholds. 4. Examine content-type and transfer-encoding headers for uploaded vs downloaded files. 5. Check storage layer (S3, blob store, etc.) for any configuration or library version changes.","proposed_test_case":"Upload files of varying sizes (100KB, 1MB, 5MB, 10MB) across multiple formats (PDF, PNG, DOCX). Download each and verify byte-for-byte integrity against the original. Identify the exact size threshold where corruption begins. This should be automated as a regression test for the upload/download pipeline.","information_gaps":["Exact file size threshold where corruption begins","Specific browser and OS versions (though unlikely to be relevant given cross-user scope)","Whether the corruption is truncation, encoding mangling, or something else (dev can check server-side)","Exact nature of the image corruption (similar pattern or different issue)"]}
